extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A thermal incubator system consisting of an insulated styrofoam box fitted with a heatbed and a fan for heat distribution, monitored by two internal temperature sensors and one external temperature sensor, all controlled by a Raspberry Pi. Its objective is to reach and maintain a target temperature inside the box irrespective of the content, making it an ideal candidate for digital twinning to model, monitor and optimize its temperature regulation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin supplies real‑time visualization of the incubator status by streaming sensor data to a time‑series database and rendering it on a dashboard; on‑demand calibration of the two thermal simulation models; anomaly detection and heat‑bed temperature estimation via a Kalman filter that fuses sensor measurements; what‑if simulations enabled through co‑simulation of historical or live device data; and a self‑adaptation loop that detects plant behaviour changes, schedules new experiments, recalibrates the model, re‑optimises controller settings, and updates the physical controller.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on a Raspberry Pi executing Python scripts that use SciPy optimisation routines for model calibration, a Kalman filter for state estimation, two experimental thermal models (Model A and Model B), a time‑series database for sensor streams, and a dashboard for real‑time plotting.  All source code, hardware specifications, and data sets are published in the open‑source repository at https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The low‑level driver periodically polls sensor data and publishes it to the RabbitMQ communication server; the controller on the Raspberry Pi subscribes to these updates, executes control logic, and publishes actuator commands back to the broker. No numeric frequency or latency specifications are provided in the documentation.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The digital twin is composed of a single instance that integrates three main components: the low‑level driver, the controller, and the RabbitMQ communication server. No mention of multiple twin instances or a decentralized architecture is evident in the documentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The digital twin is orchestrated through a set of inter‑linked components: a RabbitMQ communication server acts as the central message bus; a low‑level driver aggregates sensor data and publishes to RabbitMQ while consuming control commands; a bang‑bang controller consumes temperature data from RabbitMQ, enforces temperature limits via heating and fan actuators, and publishes control state; a calibration module (non‑linear least‑squares solver) is invoked on demand to refine the parameters of Model B; a Kalman‑filter module receives model predictions and sensor measurements from the time‑series database to perform anomaly detection and heat‑bed temperature estimation; a visualization component streams time‑series data to a dashboard; and a self‑adaptation loop schedules experiments, re‑configures the controller and filter, executes co‑simulation for what‑if studies, and iteratively optimizes controller parameters. This constellation implements the feedback loop described in the case study, integrating modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation within a single twin.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is realised through the RabbitMQ message broker, which enables decoupled, topic‑based publishing and subscribing between the low‑level driver, controller, and higher‑level analytics services. The broker is open‑source and can be deployed on‑premises or in the cloud, allowing plug‑in of additional enterprise applications or other digital twins if needed. No other external protocols or data‑exchange standards are explicitly referenced in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Two analytical simulation models are used to predict the internal temperature of the incubator.  Model A contains two free parameters and assumes that the heatbed does not absorb heat, while Model B contains four free parameters and better captures the thermal inertia of the heatbed.  Data for both models come from 3 DS18S20 temperature sensors—two inside the styrofoam box and one outside the box—plus the control‑logic and driver messages generated by a Raspberry Pi controller.  The Pi collects sensor data and controller state over time and streams it into a time‑series database, which is then visualised on a dashboard.  Calibration algorithms update the free parameters of Model B on demand using a least‑squares fitting procedure to the recorded data.  The calibrated models feed a Kalman filter that estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature, and they are also used in co‑simulation runs for what‑if analyses that inform controller optimisation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators in the incubator are an electric heatbed that supplies heat to the contents of the styrofoam box and a fan that distributes that heat evenly.  These components are operated by a Raspberry Pi executing a bang‑bang controller with a temperature setpoint of 38.5 °C and a tolerance of ±0.5 °C.  The controller defines a lower limit (LL) and an upper limit (UL) that delimit the hysteresis band, and additional parameters H and C that tune the hold time and the control response (exact numeric values are not specified in the documents).  The heatbed is switched on‑off via a digital GPIO output; the fan is driven continuously to provide air circulation.  All actuation commands originate from the Pi and can be issued remotely over the local network.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensory subsystem consists of three DS18S20 one‑wire digital temperature sensors—two located inside the incubator and one measuring the ambient temperature outside.  Each DS18S20 has an advertised accuracy of ±0.5 °C.  The Raspberry Pi reads the sensors at regular intervals through the 1‑wire bus; while the exact sampling period is not stated, readings are timestamped and forwarded in real time to the time‑series database.  No additional measurement modalities are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is ensured by an on‑demand calibration routine (section 3.3) that employs a least‑squares fit to align simulated temperature trajectories with the recorded sensor data.  Model B, with four calibrated parameters, provides a better fit than Model A because it captures the heatbed’s thermal inertia.  Validation is performed by comparing simulation outputs to measured temperatures and by evaluating fit metrics such as mean‑squared error (exact figures not provided).  Sensor uncertainty—±0.5 °C—contributes to overall model uncertainty, which is reduced by periodic recalibration.  An anomaly detection mechanism based on a Kalman filter monitors deviations of the internal temperature from the model; when anomalies are detected, a self‑adaptation loop triggers a new calibration and re‑optimises the controller.  Continuous monitoring, real‑time data logging, and on‑demand calibration constitute the quality‑assurance strategy for both models and data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature readings from the two internal and one external temperature sensors are read periodically by the low‑level driver on the Raspberry Pi. The driver publishes the raw sensor data to the RabbitMQ server, which serves as the interface between the physical twin and the digital twin. The driver uses a safe protocol to ensure sensor data integrity before forwarding.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are sent from the DT to the physical incubator through the same RabbitMQ broker. The controller, running on the Raspberry Pi, receives command messages to turn the heatbed or fan on/off. Messages are sent in a structured format (e.g., JSON) and validated by a safe protocol implemented in the low‑level driver to ensure that actuators are only commanded when safe to do so. After executing a command, the controller publishes its control state back to RabbitMQ, providing feedback and confirmation that the actuation was successful. Conflict resolution is handled by the bang‑bang controller logic, enforcing a heating duration and a waiting period so that only one actuation command is active at a time.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The system uses RabbitMQ, a lightweight message broker, as the central communication hub. All components publish/subscribe to topic exchanges over the same local network. The Docker or process details are not specified, nor are bandwidth or latency thresholds. Reliability and security specifics are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All twin software runs locally on a single on‑premise Raspberry Pi (edge) device. The Pi hosts the RabbitMQ broker, the low‑level driver, the bang‑bang controller, and a lightweight time‑series database for real‑time dashboarding. No external cloud hosting is referenced; the deployment model is a singular edge installation with the Pi acting both as physical twin controller and digital twin host. Availability and scalability are implied by the Raspberry Pi’s limited capacity, but the documents do not specify redundancy or scaling strategies.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin engineering process for the incubator begins with acquisition of temperature data from two internal probes and one external probe, transmitted by a Raspberry Pi to a central data store. The time‑series data is used to calibrate two finite‑difference thermal models: Model A, which has two free parameters representing heat‑transfer coefficients, and Model B, which has four free parameters capturing additional thermal‑inertia effects. Calibration of the models is performed automatically following the procedure outlined in section 3.3 of the referenced manuscript; the resulting parameter sets are committed to the open‑source repository <github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator>. Version control for the sensor‑reading scripts, calibration code, simulation routines, and test harnesses is handled by Git. The calibrated twin then feeds the simulated temperatures into a time‑series database (e.g., InfluxDB) while a monitoring component logs passes and failures. Model parameters are validated by comparing simulated temperature trajectories against measured values; significant deviations trigger a re‑calibration cycle. Unit tests, simulation tests, and integration tests are included in the repository to support continuous verification of twin functionality.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin delivers decision support via a real‑time dashboard that visualises incubator status, with live plots of measured and estimated temperatures. Anomaly detection is achieved with a Kalman‑filter estimator that infers the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature; the estimation error (residual) is monitored against a threshold to trigger alerts. The filter also provides heat‑bed state estimation, giving insight into the internal thermal dynamics. What‑if simulations are performed by running co‑simulations that replay historical or live data through the calibrated Model B, thereby allowing optimisation of controller parameters. A self‑adaptation loop can be triggered when a change in the system (e.g., placing a new object inside the incubator) causes anomalies: the twin automatically schedules an experiment, re‑configures the controller, records new data, recalibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, performs what‑if simulation, and reinstates the controller, thus closing the adaptation loop and preserving optimal operation.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware, software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology, we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals. 6 [https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator](https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) 16

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.


4 [https://www.rabbitmq.com/](https://www.rabbitmq.com/)


7


Figure 6: Schematic of the software deployment of the components.


The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
controller. The controller parameters are:

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
